entityDescription: Clarence-Rockland is a bilingual city in the United Counties of Prescott and Russell, in the Ottawa-Eastern region, with a population of 26,505 (2021 census), on the south bank of the Ottawa River about 39 km east of downtown Ottawa. Its largest community is Rockland, with Bourget and Clarence Creek among the inland villages. Formed in 1998 from the town of Rockland and Clarence Township, the city has a strong French-speaking majority and has grown quickly as a commuter community for the national capital, while keeping its roots as a 19th-century Ottawa River lumber town.

longDescription:
Clarence-Rockland is one of the most strongly French-speaking cities in Ontario, a bilingual community on the Ottawa River that has grown from a 19th-century lumber town into a fast-expanding suburb of the national capital. Home to 26,505 residents in 2021, it sits in the United Counties of Prescott and Russell in the Ottawa-Eastern region, about 39 km east of downtown Ottawa, with Rockland as its largest community.

The city gathers several communities under one name. Rockland lines the south bank of the Ottawa River, while Bourget, Clarence Creek, Hammond and Cheney sit on the clay plains inland. County Road 17 is the main artery, running parallel to the river and carrying commuters toward Ottawa. Larose Forest, one of Ontario's largest planted forests, spreads across the clay plains south of the city, and the riverfront offers views across to Quebec. Housing ranges from older village homes and riverfront properties in Rockland to large, recently built subdivisions filled with families priced out of the city core. The city's growth has made it one of the faster-growing municipalities in eastern Ontario.

Clarence-Rockland's economy is tied closely to Ottawa: most working residents commute west to federal government, technology and service jobs in Orléans and downtown before returning to Rockland each evening, while local retail, construction and bilingual services support the growing population. Rapid subdivision growth means many newer homes stand alongside aging village housing. {COMPANY_NAME} provides {SERVICE_LIST} for both, delivering {SERVICE} in English and serving the area's francophone majority, backed by {YEARS_IN_BUSINESS} years of local {BUSINESS_TYPE} work.

Eastern Ontario winters here are long and cold, with roughly 220 cm of snow, ice-storm risk and deep frost lines, while the region's sensitive Leda clay soils can shift and settle — a known local cause of foundation and drainage problems. Spring runoff toward the Ottawa River and frost on clay driveways add to the seasonal workload. Humid summers add more stress, and older brick homes in Rockland feel the freeze-thaw most. {COMPANY_NAME} plans {SERVICE} around these clay-plain conditions and hard winters so Clarence-Rockland homes stay protected year-round.

County Road 17 and nearby Highway 417 connect Clarence-Rockland to its neighbours. Ottawa lies about 39 km west, Russell sits roughly 28 km south, and North Glengarry is about 40 km to the southeast. That access lets {COMPANY_NAME} cover Rockland, Bourget and the surrounding villages without delay, on riverfront homes and new subdivision builds alike. Call {PHONE} for your {ESTIMATE_TYPE} estimate.
